Bwana asifiwe Turn with me to Matthew 24:9. When we read this passage, we see a God who does not abandon His people. Someone in the back says 'Amen!' and I agree with you, brother. You know, last week I was visiting a family near Fort Ternan, and they told me how the Lord provided during the maize harvest. The maize harvest had made things very hard for them, yet they testified of God's faithfulness. 

"1 Samuel 24:4" let that sink in for a moment. The congregation responds: "Bwana asifiwe!" 

Let me ask you: when was the last time you trusted God in the middle of a storm? Not after the storm. In the storm. There is a difference between praising God on the mountain and trusting Him in the valley. Bwana asifiwe Let us pray. Father, we come before You with hearts that are willing but sometimes weak. Strengthen us. Help us to trust Your Word even when our circumstances shout otherwise. In Jesus' name we pray. Amen.

Good morning, Londiani Worshippers! Before I read the text, let me tell you a story. There was a farmer in Tulwet who planted his maize right before the drought came. His neighbours laughed. But he said, 'My God will provide.' And He did not the way the farmer expected, but He did. 

Now, Genesis 25:6. This is not just a verse for Sunday. This is a verse for Monday morning when the children need school fees and the shamba has not produced enough. This is a verse for the hospital bedside. This is a verse for the marriage that feels like it is crumbling. 

Paul writes in Philippians 9:5 that nothing can separate us from the love of God. Nothing. Not unemployment. Not sickness. Not even our own doubts. Mungu akubariki, church! Will you believe that today? Shout it out if you believe it! [Congregation: Amen!] Practical application: I want every family here to write down one promise from Scripture and put it on your doorpost this week. Let the Word of God be the first thing you see every morning. Amen.

Our text today is found in 1 Thessalonians 3:18. I want to share three truths from this passage. 

First: God sees you. Even in Fort Ternan, even in your small prayer corner, He sees you. Hagar called Him 'El Roi' the God who sees. 

Second: God hears you. I know some of you are praying about the hospital visit. Do not give up. Jeremiah 19:7 reminds us that our prayers are not wasted. 

Third: God will answer. Not always the way we expect. Sometimes the answer is 'yes', sometimes 'wait', sometimes 'I have something better'. But He answers. 

Now I want us to take a moment of silence. [Pause] Whatever you are carrying today lay it at the feet of Jesus. Shall we pray together? Father, You are our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble. We trust You. We lean on You. Bwana asifiwe. In the mighty name of Jesus, amen.

Topic: Walking in Faith During Uncertain Times
Main Text: 1 Corinthians 45:4-15
Memory Verse: Daniel 2:11
Facilitator: Andrew Tum
